questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: What does git clone do? Which answer is best?
options: Only downloads the README without history
Copies a remote repository (history and files) to your machine
questions.explanation: Clone fetches a full working copy of the remote repo by default.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: git status — primary use? Which answer is best?
options: Show working tree and staging area state vs HEAD
questions.explanation: Status reports modified/staged/untracked relative to HEAD.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Staging area (index) — why does Git have it? Which answer is best?
options: Lets you craft commits from selected hunks/files before recording a snapshot
It is only for binary files
questions.explanation: The index builds the next commit deliberately.

questions.stem: In an interview setting, consider this question: git add -p — interview-useful meaning?? Pick the strongest answer.
options: Interactively stage hunks so commits stay focused
questions.explanation: Patch mode stages logical chunks for cleaner commits.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: git commit — what does it record? Which answer is best?
options: Only a diff without tree objects
A snapshot of the index with metadata (author, message, parents)
questions.explanation: Commits point to trees and parents forming history.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: HEAD in Git — concise definition? Which answer is best?
options: Pointer to the current commit (usually via current branch)
questions.explanation: HEAD names where you are in the commit graph.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Detached HEAD — what does it mean? Which answer is best?
options: HEAD points directly at a commit, not a branch tip — new commits can be orphaned
questions.explanation: Detached HEAD is fine for inspect/build; create a branch to keep new work.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: git branch -d vs -D? Which answer is best?
options: -d is safe delete (merged); -D force deletes even if unmerged
questions.explanation: -d protects unmerged work; -D forces local branch deletion.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: git checkout vs git switch — modern guidance? Which answer is best?
options: Prefer switch/restore for branch/file intent; checkout historically did both
questions.explanation: Newer commands split checkout's dual roles for clarity.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: git restore — typical job? Which answer is best?
options: Discard or unstage working tree/index changes for paths
questions.explanation: Restore manipulates working tree/index content without the old checkout overload.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: git log --oneline --graph — why use it? Which answer is best?
options: Compact visual history of commits and merges
questions.explanation: Graph log helps reason about branch topology.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: git diff vs git diff --staged? Which answer is best?
options: Unstaged working changes vs staged index vs HEAD differences
questions.explanation: Know which tree pair you are comparing: worktree, index, HEAD.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: git fetch vs git pull? Which answer is best?
options: Fetch updates remote-tracking refs; pull fetch+integrates into current branch
They are banned in corporate Git
questions.explanation: Fetch is safe update of remotes; pull also merges/rebases locally.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: origin typically means? Which answer is best?
options: The default remote name created by clone
questions.explanation: origin is conventional remote name, not magic.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: git push -u origin HEAD — useful effect? Which answer is best?
options: Publishes current branch and sets upstream tracking
questions.explanation: -u sets upstream so future push/pull know the remote branch.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Tracking branch — what does it enable? Which answer is best?
options: Convenient push/pull/status relative to a remote branch
questions.explanation: Upstream tracking links local and remote branches.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: git remote -v — shows what? Which answer is best?
options: Configured remotes and their fetch/push URLs
questions.explanation: Lists remote names and URLs. Give the interview-safe rationale clearly.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Fast-forward merge — when does it happen? Which answer is best?
options: When the target branch tip is ancestor of the source — pointer just moves
questions.explanation: FF updates the branch ref without a merge commit when history allows.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Three-way merge — conceptual inputs? Which answer is best?
options: Two branch tips plus their common ancestor to produce a merge commit
questions.explanation: Merge uses base + two tips; conflicts when both changed same hunks.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Merge conflict — correct next steps? Which answer is best?
options: Run git clean -fdx immediately as first step
Edit conflicted files, git add, then commit (or continue rebase/merge)
questions.explanation: Resolve markers, stage, complete the merge/rebase.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: git rebase — primary purpose? Which answer is best?
options: Replay commits onto another base for a linear history
questions.explanation: Rebase rewrites commit parentage onto a new tip.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Golden rule of rebase? Which answer is best?
options: Do not rebase commits already pushed and shared unless team agrees
Always rebase main publicly without coordination
questions.explanation: Rewriting published history confuses collaborators.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: git rebase -i — common uses? Which answer is best?
options: Squash, reorder, edit, or drop commits before sharing
questions.explanation: Interactive rebase cleans local history.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: git pull --rebase — why prefer sometimes? Which answer is best?
options: Keeps local commits on top of updated remote tip without merge bubbles
questions.explanation: Pull-rebase integrates with linear local history.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Cherry-pick — what does it do? Which answer is best?
options: Deletes the commit from all history
Applies a given commit's changes onto current branch as a new commit
questions.explanation: Cherry-pick copies a change set to another line of history.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: git revert — why safer than reset on shared main? Which answer is best?
options: It adds a new commit that undoes a prior change without rewriting history
It force-deletes commits from remotes silently
questions.explanation: Revert is additive and collaboration-friendly.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: git reset --soft vs --mixed vs --hard? Which answer is best?
options: Soft keeps index+worktree; mixed unstages; hard discards worktree changes too
Hard is safest for uncommitted work you need
Soft deletes files from disk always
questions.explanation: Know what each mode does to index and working tree.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Reflog — why is it a lifesaver? Which answer is best?
options: Is permanent forever on all remotes
Records recent HEAD movements so you can recover 'lost' commits
questions.explanation: Reflog helps recover from bad reset/rebase locally (time-limited).

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: git stash — typical workflow? Which answer is best?
options: Temporarily shelve dirty work to switch tasks, then stash pop/apply
questions.explanation: Stash parks uncommitted changes briefly.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Annotated vs lightweight tags? Which answer is best?
options: They behave identically in all tools
Annotated store tagger/message/date objects; lightweight are plain refs
questions.explanation: Prefer annotated tags for releases.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: git tag -a v1.0 && git push --tags — intent? Which answer is best?
options: Mark a release commit and publish tags to remote
questions.explanation: Annotated release tags are pushed explicitly.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Signed commits/tags — why in secure orgs? Which answer is best?
options: Cryptographic proof of author identity and integrity of the object
questions.explanation: Signatures support verify and compliance workflows.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: .gitignore — correct mental model? Which answer is best?
options: Patterns exclude untracked files from status/add; tracked files stay tracked
It untracks already committed secrets automatically
questions.explanation: Ignoring does not remove already-tracked paths.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Removing a secret committed by mistake — realistic advice? Which answer is best?
options: git reset --soft fixes remotes for everyone
Only add to .gitignore and you are done
Secrets expire if you amend once
Rotate the secret; history rewrite/BFG/filter-repo if needed; assume leak
questions.explanation: Treat leaked secrets as compromised; rotate and purge history if required.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: git submodule — what problem/trade-off? Which answer is best?
options: Embed another repo at a pinned commit; adds complexity for clones/updates
questions.explanation: Submodules pin external repos; teams often prefer packages instead.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: git worktree — useful for? Which answer is best?
options: Multiple working directories attached to one repo (e.g., parallel branches)
questions.explanation: Worktrees avoid multiple full clones for concurrent branch work.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Sparse checkout — when helpful? Which answer is best?
options: Working on a subset of a monorepo without populating every path
questions.explanation: Sparse checkout limits working tree paths.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Partial clone / blobless clone — benefit? Which answer is best?
options: Faster initial clone by delaying blob download until needed
questions.explanation: Partial clones optimize large repo network cost.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: hooks (pre-commit, pre-push) — purpose? Which answer is best?
options: Run on the remote only by default
Automate local policy checks before commit/push
questions.explanation: Client hooks enforce local guardrails; CI remains source of truth for shared policy.

questions.stem: Conventional commits / good messages — interview expectation?
options: Imperative, why-focused subject; optional body; consistent style for history/tooling
Paste entire diffs in the subject
questions.explanation: Clear messages aid review, bisect, and changelog tooling.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: git bisect — classic use case? Which answer is best?
options: Binary search history to find the commit that introduced a bug
Split a repo into two remotes
questions.explanation: Bisect isolates the first bad commit efficiently.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: git blame / annotate — what does it show? Which answer is best?
options: Per-line last modifying commit/author for archaeology
questions.explanation: Blame maps lines to responsible commits for debugging.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: octopus merge — meaning? Which answer is best?
options: A merge with more than two parents
questions.explanation: Octopus merges combine multiple heads (uncommon in apps).

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: ort / recursive merge strategies — practical takeaway? Which answer is best?
options: Modern Git defaults to capable strategies that handle renames; conflicts still need humans
questions.explanation: Strategy choice affects rename/detection behavior; conflicts remain possible.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: rerere — what does it do? Which answer is best?
options: Reuses recorded conflict resolutions on repeated merges/rebases
questions.explanation: rerere caches conflict resolutions for recurring merges.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: git replace / grafts (historical) — advanced purpose? Which answer is best?
options: Locally overlay alternative object relationships for history viewing without full rewrite
questions.explanation: Replace lets you view alternate history links locally.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Garbage collection (git gc) — why run? Which answer is best?
options: Pack loose objects and prune unreachable ones to keep the repo efficient
questions.explanation: GC maintains object database health.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Packfiles and deltas — performance idea? Which answer is best?
options: Git stores objects efficiently packed with delta compression
Every file is always full plaintext copies only
questions.explanation: Packs compress object storage and transfer.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Git LFS — when do you reach for it? Which answer is best?
options: Large binary assets that would bloat the normal object store
questions.explanation: LFS stores large blobs outside the main Git object graph.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Monorepo vs multi-repo — Git-centric trade-off to state? Which answer is best?
options: Monorepos simplify atomic cross-project changes but need tooling for scale; multi-repo isolates ownership
questions.explanation: Choose based on ownership, CI, and cross-cutting change frequency.
